subject: Re: 1 pg country club resume
body:
Hi agent smith,
The resumes were great, i handed them out to a couple places and people,
but i kept running into the same issue, they only start hiring at 16, so do
you think you can make me a list of places in wellington i can go to that
will most likely not mind a 15 year old.
And my other thing was that i am interested in private equity and i did
find a couple firms in wellington, do you think its worth looking into? If
so where and which ones.
thank you
-mathias littlejohn

On Sun, May 24, 2026 at 1:42 PM Duncan Littlejohn <
duncanlittlejohn727@gmail.com> wrote:

Follow image link: (https://promoted.net/bolt-cs-602)
Caption: 

I believe there is one make-or-break factor for any podcast, YouTube show, or livestream.*  It’s not about format, it’s not about structure, and it’s not about production value.

_— Francis Zierer, Lead Editor_

_*_(With one exception.)


----------



----------
## Your idea. Your code. Yours to keep.

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/139a82d5-7f9e-4edb-ba79-ee0497f2de17/unnamed.jpeg?t=1779736466)
Follow image link: (https://promoted.net/bolt-cs-602)
Caption: 

Most "no-code" tools box you in. You build the thing, then realize you can't export it, can't customize it, can't host it where you want.

Bolt.new is different. You prompt. It writes real code. You own it.

Founders are using it to validate ideas in a weekend — landing pages, internal tools, prototypes — without hiring a dev or learning to code themselves.

If you can describe what you want, you can ship it.

[**Try Bolt.new**](https://promoted.net/bolt-cs-602)

_This is an advertisement._


----------



----------
## Hanging out is what makes a show good

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/65c370f8-2350-4016-ad3d-a54a98b1e6cc/image__195_.png?t=1780062869)
Follow image link: (http://creatorspotlight.com/p/rule-one-of-making-a-show)
Caption: 

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/d06b7622-a275-476a-83f3-3947f79418de/Francis_Zierer__1_.png?t=1780063208) [By Francis Zierer, Lead Editor]
Follow image link: (https://www.linkedin.com/in/francis-zierer)
Caption: 

**Rule one of making a show: nothing beats a great hang.** This applies to podcasts, YouTube shows, and livestreaming; any long-form audio-first product.

The hosts, guests, or other people on camera must _genuinely_ _enjoy_ each other’s company. Audience enjoyment and _loyalty_ follow; **audiences want to hang out**, crave the parasocial.

Shows that fail to start from this foundation … fail.

(There is an exception, by the way: shows that are about depth of research expressed through careful narrative can be just as powerful.)

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/febce6e3-a188-4d9a-9b24-8af21c486ca8/Content_Breaks__1_.png?t=1723054756)
Caption: 



### Martinez & Mu: Why isn’t this working?

[Rob Martinez](https://www.instagram.com/robmartinez/?hl=en) and [Julian Mu](https://www.instagram.com/mubereats/?hl=en) have built large audiences (over 1 million and over half a million, respectively) for fun, engaging, well-crafted short-form videos. They came together to make long-form YouTube videos.

**They’ve shipped two shows. One hit, one flopped. The flop illuminated why the hit worked.**

The successful concept is called [_Feasting on $50_](https://www.youtube.com/@MartinezandMu/featured). In short, the two spend a day in a given city, conducting a three-leg competition (who has the best breakfast, lunch, and dinner) voted on by their Instagram followers. [Read more about it in the profile we published earlier this week](https://www.creatorspotlight.com/p/martinez-and-mu).

They’ve published 10 episodes of this show since October 2024. The average view count is 110.2k. (Not counting the [latest episode](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=w8Dm9p6X01U), out this week.)

The failed concept is called _Food Duels_. They only published two episodes in late 2025. (Earlier this year, they scrapped two fully edited and one raw episode!) The average view count is only 54k.

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/39c0ec3c-4665-4d0b-a4b9-788f1157f18c/Screenshot_2026-05-29_at_8.35.00_AM.png.png?t=1780058215)
Caption: 



**Some aspects are the same as the more successful show:**

* **The hosts:** It’s still Martinez & Mu

* **The broad subject and format:** A food and travel competition show

* **The publishing platform:** YouTube

* **The runtime:** Both shows average within 20–30 minutes

**But there are two crucial, fatal differences.**

**First, the hosts weren’t having fun. **Rob “didn’t love the places” he went to on the show, nor the decisions he made. The show required each participant to find the best version of a given food item (sausage in the Chicago episode), with both then submitting it to a third-party judge. This meant decisions weren’t based on what the hosts wanted or believed; there had to be a degree of cynicism in how they chose their submissions.

““The question was probably wrong […]

How can we showcase ourselves rather than, like — _this_ could do really well, let's go get it; okay, _this_ does really well — and we're just chasing after each other. We realized _we_ also weren't even really playing off of each other.”“ — [Julian Mu, ](https://www.creatorspotlight.com/podcast/s/tcsp/ep_112_they_have_2m_followers_but_they_re_starting_again_ft_martinez_and_mu)[_Creator Spotlight_](https://www.creatorspotlight.com/podcast/s/tcsp/ep_112_they_have_2m_followers_but_they_re_starting_again_ft_martinez_and_mu)

**Second, and more importantly, the structure failed to understand how the audience would watch.** In [_Feasting on $50_](https://www.youtube.com/@MartinezandMu/featured), there’s plenty of downtime between the three meals the show is built around. They’re in transit, talking to the camera. _The viewer doesn’t need to pay attention._

When they watched the show with an in-person audience, people loved it. They “understood all the rules and all of the nuance,” because they were giving it their full attention. But people, generally, don’t watch YouTube videos with other people. They watch them alone and distracted. 

On the flip side, when Rob watched [_Feasting on $50_](https://www.youtube.com/@MartinezandMu/featured) with his family, he thought all the “side quests" they went on would be a weakness. 

““I remember watching the show with my family and feeling like they disconnected during [the “side quests”]. And so we wanted to make _Food Duels_ a show where we were always going to be active. Where there's always something to do. There's always obstacles in our way.

We're really empowered as protagonists in _Food Duels._ But I think what ended up bearing out was that we succeeded in that goal, but that goal was not correct — because, frankly, YouTube is a little bit more of a passive viewing experience than we had envisioned. […]

**Actually, the reason that people liked **_**Feasting on $50**_** is because it felt like you're hanging out with us for a day.”**“ — [Rob Martinez, ](https://www.creatorspotlight.com/podcast/s/tcsp/ep_112_they_have_2m_followers_but_they_re_starting_again_ft_martinez_and_mu)[_Creator Spotlight_](https://www.creatorspotlight.com/podcast/s/tcsp/ep_112_they_have_2m_followers_but_they_re_starting_again_ft_martinez_and_mu)

YouTube, podcasts, and streaming are passive-viewing channels. They’re for background attention while eating lunch, working out, washing dishes, and what-have-you.

Shows that are all peak, with no valleys, punish the audience.

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/febce6e3-a188-4d9a-9b24-8af21c486ca8/Content_B
